content:
# HTS Chapter 84 — Machinery

Quick triggers:
- Pumps -> 8413, compressors -> 8414
- If it does mechanical work, start in 84 not 85.

GRI reminder: classify by function, then essential character.

# HTS Chapter 85 — Electrical

- Motors -> 8501, transformers -> 8504
- Wiring sets for vehicles -> 8544.30
- Watch the 84 vs 85 line: powered by electricity is not enough.

# HTS Chapter 87 — Vehicle parts

- Parts of motor vehicles -> 8708 (most Meridian/Great Lakes work)
- Brakes -> 8708.30, transmissions -> 8708.40
- Note Section 232/301 overlays before quoting duty.

# HTS Chapters 61/62 — Apparel

- 61 = knit, 62 = woven. Determine construction first.
- Fiber content drives the subheading and the duty rate.
- Buckeye Textile cotton tees -> 6109.10.

# HTS Chapter 12 — Oilseeds

- Soybeans -> 1201, soybean meal -> 2304
- Heartland Ag exports: confirm whole vs broken beans.
- Watch FDA and USDA overlays on ag commodities.

# GRI 1-6 quick reference

1. Terms of headings and notes govern.
2. Incomplete/unfinished and mixtures.
3. Most specific, then essential character, then last in numerical order.
4. Most akin.
5. Cases and packing.
6. Subheadings, same rules.

# Section 232 vs 301

- 232 = national security (steel/aluminum + derivatives).
- 301 = China-origin, list-based, exclusion-driven.
- Always check both before quoting a landed cost.

# Customs valuation

- Transaction value is the default.
- Additions: assists, royalties, packing, commissions.
- First sale possible with bona fide sale for export and docs.

# USMCA rules of origin

- Tariff shift vs regional value content.
- Autos: net cost method, rising RVC thresholds.
- Keep supplier origin statements on file for 5 years.

# ADD/CVD red flags

- Scope rulings matter more than the HTS itself.
- Watch transshipment and country-of-origin claims.
- Check the case number against the latest scope.

# Great Lakes shipping still shapes Midwest trade

Spent the morning thinking about why so much of my client base sits within a day of a Great Lakes port.
Toledo, Cleveland, and the St. Lawrence Seaway still move grain and steel cheaper than rail for the right loads.

For Heartland Ag and Maumee Valley, the lake routes are the whole ballgame in season.

# Where I think tariffs are heading

Not a prediction, just a working view. The trend is more product-specific actions, fewer broad cuts.
That means classification work gets harder and more valuable, not less.

Plan: build out the cheat sheets so Brandon can carry more of the routine calls.

# Running a six-person firm

The hard part is not the trade law, it's keeping six people pointed the same direction without meetings eating the week.
Marcus and Priya can run their own files now. Donna keeps the books clean. Pat holds the front.

Brandon is the long game.

# What the mentorship program is really for

Ryan and I started it to pay forward the breaks we got. It is not lead gen and it is not billable.
The payoff is a better bench of brokers in Columbus, which lifts everyone.

# Classification is judgment

A search tool gets you to a chapter. The headings and notes get you the rest of the way, and that is where the experience lives.
Essential character is a habit of mind, not a rule you memorize.
